https://github.com/godotengine/godot/com.commit_message:
Merge pull request #114190 from voylin/fix_crash_scriptless_scene_debug

Fix error when break scriptless scene

description.text:
Refactor ScriptDebugger (followup on #36244).
EngineDebugger is the new interface to access the debugger.
It tries to be as agnostic as possible on the data that various subsystems can expose.

It allows 2 types of interactions:

- Profilers:
  A subsystem can register a profiler, assigning it a unique name.
  That name can be used to activate the profiler or add data to it.
  The registered profiler can be composed of up to 3 functions:
    - Toggle: called when the profiler is activated/deactivated.
    - Add: called whenever data is added to the debugger
      (via `EngineDebugger::profiler_add_frame_data`)
    - Tick: called every frame (during idle), receives frame times.

- Captures: (Only relevant in remote debugger for now)
  A subsystem can register a capture, assigning it a unique name.
  When receiving a message, the remote debugger will check if it starts
  with `[prefix]:` and call the associated capture with name `prefix`.

Port MultiplayerAPI, Servers, Scripts, Visual, Performance to the new profiler system (the goal is to keep profiling code into the debugger folder, so it doesn't pollute the engine and can be completely disabled on release builds if so desired).

Port SceneDebugger and RemoteDebugger to the new capture system.
The LocalDebugger also uses the new profiler system for scripts
profiling.

Some considerations:
- **Since remote debugger now uses threads for networking**, we should review some properties like
  - max errors
  - max messages
  - max chars per seconds.
- We could reconsider some debugger features like F8 to quit the debugged game (I didn't even know we had that).
- As a next step, I'll finally integrate #33925
- **Should I rename EngineDebugger to CoreDebugger?**
- **Should I rename various `Editor*Profiler` to `EditorDebugger*Profiler`?**
- Captures are a bit specific, and maybe we could bind properties/capabilities instead.
  IIRC @Calinou and @fire suggested looking into [DAP](https://microsoft.github.io/debug-adapter-protocol/), while that seems to be a long shot, I hope this work goes into the direction of splitting different debug components apart and could potentially help in achieving such a goal (I really don't feel like working on that right now, my brain is exploding after this work :face_with_head_bandage:, sorry).

*Note*: the 1000 extra lines is the threading code, not the refactor, which in fact resulted in the same amount of code (few extra header license lines), but moved it away, whenever possible, from engine components and into the debugger folder.

ranked_scenarios.summary: Fix errors not being emitted when debugger breaks on script errors

description.text:
Fixes #107662.

This does the following:

1. Errors are now emitted when the built-in debugger breaks on a script error, allowing `Logger` to intercept such errors.
2. Effectively changes the location of this emitted error (which applies to the "Ignore Error Breaks" scenario as well) from `RemoteDebugger::debug` to instead be the actual script location.

closed_issues.summary: `Logger` methods are not called when debugger breaks on script error

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in: 4.5.dev [46c495ca21f40f57a7fb9c7cde6143738f1652d4]

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (46c495ca2) - Fedora Linux 42 (Adams) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon Graphics (RADV GFX1201) - AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 60.39 GiB memory

### Issue description

The new `Logger` class that was introduced in #91006 is meant to be able to capture all errors and log messages for as long as its registered. However, if you're playing your game through the editor, and haven't enabled the "Ignore Error Breaks" toggle in the Debugger panel, any GDScript error (like accessing a null reference) will only trigger a debugger break but won't actually emit any error message for `Logger` to pick up on.

When looking at the code, the suppressing of these error messages seems to be deliberate:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/46c495ca21f40f57a7fb9c7cde6143738f1652d4/core/debugger/remote_debugger.cpp#L106-L109
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/46c495ca21f40f57a7fb9c7cde6143738f1652d4/core/debugger/remote_debugger.cpp#L412-L429

This might have made sense before the introduction of `Logger`, but seeing as how your `Logger` could be set up to forward errors to some error reporting service or wherever, I would argue that it becomes somewhat problematic for `RemoteDebugger` to be suppressing errors like this.

I would maybe go so far as to argue that the current behavior isn't necessarily intuitive either.

There's also another intertwined issue here, which is that the `ERR_PRINT(error_str)` seen above, which the "Ignore Error Breaks" toggle makes use of in order to let through the previously suppressed error, results in the location of the error being misreported, since it always reports `RemoteDebugger::debug` as the location rather than the script it actually originated from.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open the MRP.
2. Run the main scene.
3. Note how the debugger breaks at the null access.
4. Note how there's no error in the "Errors" tab in the "Debugger" panel.
5. Note how `MyLogger` hasn't printed anything.
6. Enable the "Ignore Error Breaks" toggle, under the "Stack Trace" tab in the "Debugger" panel.
7. Run the main scene again.
8. Note how the debugger doesn't break at the null access.
9. Note how there is an error in the "Errors" tab in the "Debugger" panel.
10. Note how `MyLogger` has printed an error.
11. Note how the location of the error is incorrect.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[logger-script-breaks.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20793157/logger-script-breaks.zip)

* Continuation of: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/77945

The primary reason is plain correctness, but several of these cases, especially the wider cases that will be added soon, are hard to tell what they should be with the current code, so having an explicit null check makes reading the code easier

The most straight-forward argument, taken from `display_server_web.cpp`:
```cpp
ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(!tts, false, "Enable the \"audio/general/text_to_speech\" project setting to use text-to-speech.");
```
And the one from `display_server_windows.cpp`:
```cpp
ERR_FAIL_COND_V_MSG(!tts, false, "Enable the \"audio/general/text_to_speech\" project setting to use text-to-speech.");
```
Only one is a null check, despite being entirely identical otherwise.

Edit: Also cleaned up some error messages for style when they were already part of the change.
